headline: Riley’s answers were mostly 1-2 sentence fragments about missing decision-making and ownership context, creating a partial read rather than a full onboarding signal.
summary_bullets: Riley can trace request paths and read services, but he still lacks the “historical context behind certain tradeoffs” that explains why decisions were made.
Ownership and escalation are the main friction points: “who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict” and “what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.”
He’s self-suppressing questions to avoid interrupting: “sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking,” which delays feedback and calibration.
understanding_paragraph: Riley is progressing through codebase orientation, but his mental model is missing how decisions get made and who owns what when priorities collide. He’s actively trying to be considerate (“not to interrupt people”), yet that behavior is pushing questions into a notes doc instead of live calibration. The onboarding gap isn’t effort—it’s the lack of fast, practical context loops (especially around incidents) and clear ownership boundaries.

axes.meaning: Weak signal tied to onboarding stress from unclear incident/ownership expectations; worth watching over the next few weeks.
He’s motivated but constrained by uncertainty; he wants clearer ownership so he can contribute with confidence.
Defining signal: decision-making, domain ownership under conflict, and escalation-vs-batch rules are unclear.
Very weak positive signal: he named a concrete next step to ask an architecture question in an incident review.

brutal_truth_employee: Riley said he is “less sure how decisions are really made here” and that he “has not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.” He also admitted he “keep[s] questions in a notes doc instead of asking,” which means he’s delaying the exact context he says he needs.
brutal_truth_manager: Riley offered clear, actionable friction (“historical context,” “who is on point,” and “escalate quickly versus batch”), but the conversation didn’t lock those into a concrete escalation path and named owners. The moment to deepen was when he said he keeps questions in a notes doc—next time, push for “who should I ask instead, and what’s the expected response time?” so the pattern stops.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute pairing with Riley to map domain ownership and decision-making: list owners per domain and who decides during priority conflicts.
Set up a weekly architecture review for Riley with a senior engineer and confirm the agenda: tradeoffs, decision history, and “what to ask in incidents.”
Confirm Riley’s escalation-vs-batch guide: ask him to describe one real incident example and whether he escalated or batched, then adjust the rules together.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Riley still routes questions into a notes doc instead of asking during reviews/incidents, treat it as a continuing context-delivery blocker.
Before next 1:1: if Riley can’t name the owner for a domain conflict and the expected escalation timing, the onboarding clarity gap is still active.
